title: Typosquatting Campaign Plants Credential Stealers in Popular npm Packages
excerpt: Researchers documented a wave of malicious npm packages using names close to widely-used libraries to trick developers into installing credential-stealing code.
summary: A typosquatting campaign published look-alike npm packages that exfiltrate environment variables and tokens on install. Developers are advised to pin dependencies, verify package names, and audit lockfiles. This is a demonstration summary — attribute and read the original.

title: Ransomware Crew Drops Encryption, Moves to Pure Data-Extortion Model
excerpt: An established ransomware operation is reportedly abandoning file encryption in favor of stealing data and threatening publication to pressure victims into paying.
summary: A prominent ransomware group is pivoting from encryption to steal-and-leak extortion, simplifying operations while increasing pressure on victims. Defenders should prioritize exfiltration detection and egress monitoring. Demonstration summary — see the source for details.

title: CISA Warns of Actively Exploited RCE in Enterprise VPN Appliances
excerpt: A newly disclosed unauthenticated remote code execution flaw in a widely deployed VPN appliance is being exploited in the wild, prompting an emergency advisory.
summary: An unauthenticated RCE affecting enterprise VPN gateways is under active exploitation; patches and mitigations are available. Organizations should patch immediately, hunt for indicators of compromise, and rotate credentials. Demonstration summary — read the advisory.

title: Phishing Kit Abuses OAuth Consent to Bypass MFA at Scale
author: Brian Krebs
excerpt: A phishing-as-a-service kit tricks users into granting malicious OAuth application permissions, sidestepping multi-factor authentication for persistent mailbox access.
summary: A phishing kit weaponizes OAuth consent screens to gain durable mailbox access without stealing passwords, evading MFA. Admins should restrict third-party app consent and review granted permissions. Demonstration summary — attribute to the source.

title: Misconfigured Object Storage Exposes Millions of Records Across SaaS Vendors
excerpt: Security researchers found dozens of publicly accessible cloud storage buckets leaking customer records due to overly permissive access policies.
summary: Publicly exposed object-storage buckets leaked millions of records across multiple SaaS providers due to misconfigured access controls. Teams should enforce least-privilege policies and enable public-access blocking. Demonstration summary — read the original.

title: Deep Dive: A Use-After-Free Zero-Day in a Mainstream Browser Engine
excerpt: A technical write-up analyzes a use-after-free vulnerability in a browser rendering engine that was exploited before a patch was released.
summary: A detailed analysis walks through a use-after-free zero-day in a browser engine, the exploitation primitive, and the vendor fix. Users should update browsers promptly. Demonstration summary — see the full research.

title: New Cyber-Resilience Rules Set Baseline Security Duties for Connected Devices
excerpt: Regulators outlined new baseline security obligations for manufacturers of connected products, including vulnerability handling and update commitments.
summary: Incoming regulation establishes minimum security duties for connected-device makers, covering secure defaults, vulnerability disclosure, and update guarantees. Vendors should map obligations to their SDLC now. Demonstration summary — read the source.

title: A New Class of HTTP Request Smuggling Against Modern Front-Ends
excerpt: Researchers present a novel request-smuggling technique that desynchronizes modern reverse proxies from back-end servers, enabling cache poisoning and access control bypass.
summary: New research demonstrates an HTTP request-smuggling variant that desyncs front-end proxies from origins, enabling cache poisoning and auth bypass. Teams should test proxies and normalize request parsing. Demonstration summary — read the research.

title: On the Promise and Peril of AI-Assisted Malware Triage
author: Bruce Schneier
excerpt: A commentary weighs how large language models can accelerate malware triage while cautioning about over-reliance, hallucinated indicators, and adversarial evasion.
summary: An essay examines using AI to speed up malware triage, balancing productivity gains against risks like hallucinated indicators and adversarial evasion. Analysts should treat AI output as a lead, not ground truth. Demonstration summary — read the commentary.
